sound_type: deep resonant booming/thumping -- extremely low-frequency sounds produced by inflating cervical air sacs, 50-100 Hz fundamental, more felt than heard, the lowest-frequency bird vocalization in the PNW; E=5
ecosystem: Conifer slopes -- Douglas-fir, true fir, spruce forests at middle to high elevations. Interior mountains east of the Cascade crest. Subalpine and montane forest. Reverse altitudinal migration: upslope in winter.
season: Year-round resident. Booming display April-June. Reverse migration: upslope in autumn, downslope in spring.
family: Phasianidae (2nd entry. SECOND PHASIANIDAE. Formerly Blue Grouse -- interior populations.)

coordinates.description: Dusky Grouse in interior mountain forests of Washington -- east side of Cascades. Douglas-fir, ponderosa pine, true fir zones. Klickitat, Yakima, Chelan, Okanogan Counties.

taxonomy_note.description: SECOND Phasianidae entry. Formerly Blue Grouse -- split in 2006 by AOU into Dusky Grouse (interior, D. obscurus) and Sooty Grouse (coastal, D. fuliginosus). Dusky at 154, Sooty at 156. Evidence: genetic divergence, vocalization differences, air sac coloration (purplish-red vs. yellow), plumage. Cascade crest as approximate boundary.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Phasianidae: second entry. Wild Turkey (153) + Dusky Grouse (154). Ruffed Grouse (155) and Sooty Grouse (156) follow.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Dendragapus from Greek 'dendron' (tree) + 'agapos' (loving) -- tree-loving. obscurus from Latin 'dark, dusky' -- referring to the plumage color.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Dendragapus -- first entry. Two species after split: Dusky Grouse (D. obscurus) and Sooty Grouse (D. fuliginosus).
taxonomy_note.order_note: Galliformes. Second entry (after Wild Turkey at 153).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Second Phasianidae. Interior split of Blue Grouse. Subsonic booming display. Lowest frequency PNW bird vocalization.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Booming: approximately 50-100 Hz fundamental. Partially infrasonic. Lowest frequency bird vocalization in the PNW.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 50-100 Hz. Extremely low. Near the lower limit of human hearing (20 Hz).
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Booming: 50-100 Hz. Harmonics may extend to 200-400 Hz but with much less energy.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual note: ~0.3-0.5 seconds. Bout: 5-7 notes over ~8-12 seconds. Sessions: repeated bouts over minutes to hours.
song_metrics.note_structure: BOOMING: Deep resonant 'whoom' or 'boop'. Produced by air forced through syrinx from inflated cervical air sacs. Helmholtz resonance determines frequency.
song_metrics.note_types: Booming (primary, breeding display -- vocal/pneumatic), clucking (contact/alarm -- vocal), whining (female nesting -- vocal).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Display bout: 5-7 deep booming notes at ~1-2 second intervals. Repeated bouts during display session.
song_metrics.timbre: Deep, resonant, thumping. More felt than heard. Physical vibration in chest. Uncanny, almost seismic quality. Not easily described as 'sound' -- more like a pressure pulse.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. Not conventionally loud (moderate dB SPL). Energy concentrated in extremely low frequencies felt as physical vibration. Effective range: 100-300 meters.
song_metrics.tempo: 5-7 notes per bout, spaced ~1-2 seconds apart. Bouts repeated at irregular intervals during display sessions.
song_metrics.context: Breeding season (April-June). Dawn and early morning. Male territorial/courtship display from elevated site or fallen log.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Dusky Grouse booming is slightly higher-pitched and more rapid than Sooty Grouse booming -- a diagnostic difference between the split species.
song_metrics.key_property: DEEP BOOMING -- 50-100 Hz, lowest frequency PNW bird, more felt than heard, cervical air sac Helmholtz resonance, purplish-red air sacs, 5-7 note bouts. E=5. SECOND PHASIANIDAE. Formerly Blue Grouse (interior). Split from Sooty (156) in 2006. Reverse altitudinal migration.

plumage.overall: Males: blue-gray overall, slightly paler and grayer than Sooty Grouse. Purplish-red cervical air sacs surrounded by white feather rosettes during display. Females: mottled brown for camouflage.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Males: purplish-red air sacs (diagnostic vs. Sooty's yellow). White feather rosettes. Blue-gray body. Females: mottled brown.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Air sacs only prominently visible during breeding display. Otherwise concealed.
plumage.flight_pattern: Burst flight when flushed. Short-distance. Strong but brief.

physical_description.overall: Length: 43-51 cm. Weight: 850-1300 g. Large grouse. Similar in size to Sooty Grouse. Males slightly larger than females.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Blue-gray body (paler than Sooty). Purplish-red air sacs in display. Interior mountain habitat (east of Cascade crest in WA/OR).
physical_description.field_id: Large gray grouse in interior mountain forest. Listen for deep booming -- more felt than heard. Extremely difficult to locate by sound alone due to low frequency.

nodes.title: Subsonic Booming -- Lowest Frequency PNW Bird
nodes.description: 50-100 Hz fundamental. Partially infrasonic. More felt than heard. Chest vibration before ear detection. Cervical air sacs as Helmholtz resonators. Extremely difficult to localize in forest environment.

nodes.title: Helmholtz Resonance Mechanism
nodes.description: Cervical air sacs function as Helmholtz resonators -- cavities that amplify specific low frequencies. Inflation reveals purplish-red skin surrounded by white feather rosettes. Frequency determined by cavity volume and aperture.

nodes.title: Blue Grouse Taxonomic Split (2006)
nodes.description: AOU split Blue Grouse into Dusky (interior, purplish-red air sacs, D. obscurus) and Sooty (coastal, yellow air sacs, D. fuliginosus). Evidence: genetics, vocalizations, air sac color, plumage. Engine: Dusky at 154, Sooty at 156.

nodes.title: Reverse Altitudinal Migration
nodes.description: Upslope in winter (opposite of most mountain birds). Conifer needle diet at higher elevation. Snow roosting for insulation. Solitary and sedentary in winter -- perching in individual conifers for weeks.

cross_references.relationship: SPLIT PAIR! Formerly one species (Blue Grouse). Dusky (154, interior, purplish-red) and Sooty (156, coastal, yellow). Same genus Dendragapus.
PHASIANIDAE CONTRAST! Turkey gobble (153, E=6, audible 1+ mile) vs. Dusky Grouse booming (154, E=5, 50-100 Hz, felt not heard). Loudest to lowest.
GROUSE TRIO! Dusky booming (154, vocal), Ruffed drumming (155, mechanical), Sooty booming (156, vocal). Different species, different mechanisms.
LOW-FREQUENCY PAIR! Both produce low-frequency sounds that penetrate dense forest. Different mechanisms (vocal vs. mechanical) but shared acoustic niche.
